pages.text:
86 Westfield Rd, Edgbaston, Birmingham, B153 JG.

[glued-in postcard of Chenonceau — printed text on postcard: CHENONCEAU]

Birthday present from Peter
June 6th 1989.

__Camping in the van__. May 1989....

.... October 1992

Van Picked up by Jim, Margaret, Helen,
      Simon, Thomas, William, Peter.

1 Malvern+ Farm 1989
2 Harlech
4 Peak District
5 Dinas
6 Leeds
7 York

[illegible] [?1st] [?Camp] [illegible]
[illegible]
[illegible]

__May.__
First we spent a day having a picnic at __Malvern__, visiting a quilting exhibition.
Then we went to Kate & Jamie's __farm__ for one night, very warm and humid.
The van worked well. The boys played on bikes + the water shoot.
We forgot a first aid kit.

When first Simon showed the van to me, Peter was sprayed by the tap.

headings: Half-term at Dinas Camp near Harlech. May
pages.text:
Half-term at __Dinas Camp__ near __Harlech__. May
Simon 39, Eve 35, Thomas 9, William 7,
Peter 6 (just) John 21months, Charles 6 weeks-

Lovely campsite in the hills.
Rubber tyre for the boys to swing on.
East wind tent. .
We forgot the can opener.
The boys played ball with Liverpool family,
including man in a wheelchair.
Eve backed into a wall.
Simon carried John in his push chair
all the way up to the castle at __Harlech__ from
the carpark. The van worked beautifully. Hot
showers, comfortable bed.

        We visited the river for swimming,
__Harlech__ castle, sandy beach (including sandy
icecream) and had a picnic at the Roman
steps.

        On walks, Simon carried John on his
[bottom of page cut off] Eve carried Charles in Phyllida's

carrier, Thomas carried the food
(beef sandwiches, apple, choc bar, water)
and William sometimes carried the
spare clothes.

Almost Contenious work, glorious sunshine
+ a fabulous holiday — even time to
read + write letters

        Simon was very nervous about Eve's
driving, and only stopped concentrating for
her when Peter bit into a "blood" sweet in
the back seat....

                                        June 10th
Malvern old boys' day. A very useful base
here. Joined Robert Upex. Mrs. Hill at
No1.

headings: July 1st. Weekend in the Peak District
pages.text:
July 1st. Weekend in the __Peak District__
We all slept in the van at Hopping Farm.
Used chemical loo for the first time. It worked well.
	Walk into Alport, (pretty, running water, trout, damp,) + Youlgreave.  Well-dressing last week, but didn't see any.
	Checked on bike-hire for a later trip cycling along Tissington Trail.
	Haddon Hall (mediaeval) closed, alas.
__Chatsworth__
	Glorious Capability Brown landscape.
Paxton was head gardener.
	Cascades. Large pebbles from Eastbourne for [mas] mosaic snake set in pavement.

July 14th Friday
Off to Dinas Camp near __Harlech__ again, to join Tim, Margaret, Helen and David, Sally, Maia. We left before breakfast & ate at the cricket pitch.
Wednesday. Glorious weather again, every meal eaten outdoors. The 4 boys took it in turns to have breakfast with Grandma.
On the Saturday we went on a very energetic walk across country, carrying Charles miles in my arms across heather & blueberry bushes. 7 miles.
Grandma finds it very hot.
We walked along a beautiful valley & swam in the river.
We visited Portmadoc — Sunny Italian architecture, but woods not as good as Welsh hill sides. Found a shop — Cara, to sell Fuschia sweater.
Visiting Alternative Technology Centre on the way home.
Simon cooked 2 chickens on the barbecue.

John's birthday. Saturday Aug 19th
  Off to the north. Leeds - camped on the Sykes front drive. Swimming with NCT + watched canoeists afterwards. Catherine is just able to stand. We all had chocolate birthday cake.
Sun After St. Aidan's church we drove to York.
  We arrived at the Minster just after the start of evensong. Crowds carefully shut away from Christian worship. Officious officials. Scaffolding for thunderbolt repairs. Disappointing
  We rode in a horse + cart around York. &. Stone column upside down. The Mayor's House, where we later sat on the steps.
Mon We spent the night in a good campsite at __Bishopsthorpe__. There's a Marina. We [?much] took a Riverboat into and out of York. A lovely, lovely bishop's house/palace on the river. We walked around the Shambles. (Butcher's Fleshammels) The __Castle Museum__'s marvellous streets (enclosed) reconstructed streets filled with Dr. Kirk's "Bygones". (Thomas) (Merrygoround with

pages.sketch_desc: Two small sketches appear on the right page: one near the top showing what appears to be a domed/rounded structure (possibly a tent or a fairground ride), and one near the middle-right showing a simplified drawing of Clifford's Tower on its mound.
pages.text:
horse shoofte
Gilligan's
Merrygoround.
Knights and boy + girl in Edwardian dress.
(William) big block, horse + carriage, 1989 Kitchen
(Peter) - in the Shambles - playing lute, fire engine
                                    stuffed dog
(Simon) lights from past + present, Ewbank washing machine, development of kitchens.
(Eve) 2 seats side-by-side of farm loo, carved in wooden bench. Anglo-Saxon mask, Jacobean livingroom - ornately carved cradle, carpenter's frame marks, no cushions or carpets.
  Cliford's tower on man-built mound.

  We walked around the Shambles, found a loo
Sir Peter + the children watched a video on the history of York, while I fed Charles + Simon [looked] around the display.
  We came back on the boat the day after the Thames Riverboat accident. There were not enough life jackets or emergency exits on the boat

On both Sunday and Monday we managed to be ready to leave by 10. We get up at about 7.30. Lay the table, eat breakfast, wash up [illegible] breast feed Charles, solid feed him, change his nappy, change John's nappy. Put away sleeping bags, tables & chairs & tent.

Tues
    We left camp (after dogshit problems by fresh-water pipe) & found delicious Blackberries in the hedges at our coffee break, which we picked for lunch. We're on our way to __Hadrian's Wall__. Campsite lovely open field, but very windy Stonehaugh * * * * *
Chesters
William - "Daddy, why aren't people harmed by radio waves?"

Wed
    Shopping in __Hexham__, Simon, Thomas & William went to Vindolanda; Peter, John, Charles & Eve rested. Very windy night. Thomas & Peter evacuated tent at 4.am.
Thu Very wet walk to Housesteads in the after-noon. Simon bought Roman book presents for

the boys, a sewing-kit for Eve & Family Membership of National Heritage.
Thomas:- "Daddy, will I catch Huntingdon's Chorea just from looking at a picture of it? 'No, but looking at a picture of Jesus might make me more holy'"
Peter used his telescope from a lookout. Tried to dry out clothes on fierce van heater going down the motorway. We arrived at Wood End campsite, * * * * * near Austwick, near __Settle__, Yorkshire, quite sodden & all slept in the van. Charles, aged 4 months, is sleeping on a folded towel on the kitchen floor, & being bathed in the sink.
Friday One of the best days of the holiday. We went on a 3 mile walk up the Currick Valley, near Ribblesdale. Up lanes, only bridleways walled in by high drystone walls on both sides. Slow walking, lots of blackberries, streams & bridges, my favourite sort of walk. Simon carried Charles on his front & John on his back. John walked, reluctantly, then Simon

pages.sketch_desc: Two hand-drawn sketches of dry-stone walls: one showing Northumberland style (double row of protruding stones) and one showing North Yorkshire style (built-in stiles, under-holes for sheep, tall ladders for hikers).
pages.text:
went ahead & picked a few berries, respacing them on thorns lower down the bush, for John to pick his own, whereupon John's slow plod changed into a jog.
	Postman Pat country - we even saw a van. Many bridleways for walkers.
	Walls in Northumberland
often have a double row of sticking-out stones:-

[sketch of Northumberland wall with double row of protruding stones]

Whereas the ones here in North Yorkshire have built-in stiles, and under-holes for sheep, with tall-ladders for hikers.
[inserted above: longstones right through]

[sketch of North Yorkshire wall with stile, under-hole, and tall ladder]

Straight after lunch we went to Mr. Hird's riding pony trekking, for the boys, but he didn't have any space. He's a cousin of the campsite

farmer. So we went shopping in Settle instead, looking for places to sell "Decorum"
	We found an interesting shop - Holland & Daughter. (but she's married now) The owner sells hand knitwear from [strikethrough] Aran a rare breed of sheep. She might sell our Arans. She rowed with a woman on the telephone who had sent her a "hard-sell" brochure - "We're not like that in the Lakes."
	We bought delicious cakes & pies at the bakery.
	The farm shop on the campsite sells lovely fresh milk, Wensleydale cheese, eggs & cream & orange juice. Loo clean.
	The farmer & his wife were very nice. He was very friendly & patient explaining about the milking. We saw the cows being brought down for milking & watched the milking in the milking parlour - 6 at a time.
	Thomas & William sent out to sleep in the tent. Much fighting among Thomas, William & Peter.
Sat We are stopping in at Burnley to see

Mr. Rourke's factory on the way home. Much rain. __Burnley__
Wrought iron - forges & heavy hammer pounding red hot iron into a shape.
Another pushing machine puts a bend in the metal.
A third machine puts a scroll turn, spinning round, gripping the end.
To make gates & bedheads & ornate window bars.

After Burnley, we drove to __Warburton__ in Cheshire. St. Werburgh's church is falling into disrepair; it is a Redundant Church — black & white timber frame 1645, stone and 1711 brick, with a modern brick vestry, ugly; from 1927. [illegible] there at

the turn of the century.
	The cemetery has tombstones of Warburtons, William 1600 or 1700, and this century George & John Warburtons. One Warburton tombstone marked 4 dead children, too.
	Afterwards we went to __Arley Hall__, which the Warburtons moved to in about 1400.
	It is now lived in by Lord & Lady Ashbrook. In 17[illegible]. the Egertons married the Warburtons. In about 1937 the Ashbrooks married the last Miss Egerton-Warburton.
	We met the family, & I asked them if they would like a photo of our picture of Arley Hall. They seemed very nice, (so did the staff) & he had corresponded with Piers, via Sotherbys, to

buy two pictures which had hung in the Hall 200 years ago.

September 29th.
	We went to stay with __Venetia__. David was cutting the grass for his 40° birthday. Venetia had been to France the weekend before + was off to Spain for George's wedding the weekend after.
	Simon helped cut the grass. Thomas helped George muck out the bunny.
	David took the boys to watch George play football. They climbed St. Catherie's Hill, which featured in William's book.
	Gala spent the weekend tied to the top because of chicken-chasing. Archie is gorgeous. Alas, Olivia wasn't there - at boarding school.
	Harvest Festival in little Somborne

church. A hymn about the rain bow.
	We stopped off at Robert Welch's workshop in Chipping Camden on the way home - green light in the window.
	Fuschia arans in a woollen shop, so we made contact for Decorum.

October 28th Saturday
We have set off for the last trip of the year - a night in Shropshire. Stokesay Castle. There's a warning of rain + gales.
	We visited Natalie Hodgson at Astley Abbots on the way but she was out.
	__Stokesay castle__ is a (13) fortified Manor House. It's very pretty, even in the rain. Lovely stonework, round rooms similar to Orford Castle, built by a woolmerchant

Lord + Lady's solar chamber, battlements. Cellar with barrel marks on walls. Green, cosy. You can imagine a family living there - it would be a challenge to make it comfortable for a family today, after 250 years without residents.
	Arrived at __Hamperley__ campsite ****
Lovely big flat field, wood along one side. Extraordinary loo, put together free - corrugated, brown paper over floor, Space also used to store old cooker + T.V. Very useful because only 1¼ hours from B'ham. Very windy during the night.
	In the morning we visited Ludlow Castle. The boys waved from the battlements.. We walked around [?Ludlow].
	The year finished with a [illegible] at [?Tomislen].

1990

April 6th. Day out to Windmill + Chur[ch] with Doris + Adelbert,
	Simon, John, Charles + I came home a quick route, because of [chi]lds, leaving Doris + Adelbert + Thomas + Peter to Adelbert's map-

April 20th.
We set off + left John + Charles with Grandma + Grandpa. Then on to Di + Ian at, [?Nortom] [?Combe], [Waterbourne written above in different ink/annotation]. We stayed the night in their garden, using the bathroom in the bungalow. Great! We went for ~~The next~~ a walk up Windmill Hill.
	The next morning we saw the Great Barn museum at __Avebury__, + walked around the Standing Stones. The wood turner made a rattle for Charles.
	In the afternoon we walked up to West Kennet Long Barrow, into the Neolithic Burial Chamber.
	We drove on and looked around __Devizes__. Antique Book Shop, drinks. We camped at the Lakeside, Rowde ***. Lots of water, glad to have left John + Charles behind.
	In the evening we walked a mile along the foot path up to the Bear for supper

At the top of the field a tree had blown over + we had to climb up 10ft to get over it. Simon + I had home made apple pie.
	The weather is cold – a cold wind.
April 21st.
	At Rowde a man lived who was later burned as a heretic at Salisbury in the reign of Bloody Mary.
	We went to Bowden Hill, St. Anne, [?lay reader] for Church BCP communion. Sermon too long. Then on to __Lacock__, museum for
	Fox-Talbot, inventor of photography 1839. He inherited the Abbey when he was 27 in 1827. Not only was he [?able] in cuneic script, Physics, Maths ([?1/2]" Wrangler) + photo chemistry, he was also a magistrate + MP, working for the

Reform Act, so he served local people. His grand daughter left the Abbey to the N.T. in 1944.
    We walked around Lacock, very pretty, weavers' houses. Church closed.
    We had lunch in Lacock carpark, & read afterwards.
    __Bradford__ also has beautiful stonehouses, 2 churches, one a lovely Saxon chapel. A delicious cream tea at the Bridge Tea Room. Violin with Lion head.
    On towards Bath, we are spending the night at __Stowford Farm,__ west of Trowbridge. Originally a mill, again lots of water, very, very pretty. Cows, goats, hens, peacocks. Clean loos.

__Bath__ Peter map read. We've brought Thomas a plaster angel with guitar. Seen 8 dining room chairs.
    We visited the Roman Baths with

the boys, Julius, Victorian statue replaced. little water channel came in to Great Bath from the Sacred Spring (Aquae Sulis, & Minerva) & you could walk over it on a little bridge. Floor mosaics. Head of Minerva. Original cooler bath built too low, flooded by Avon, so Romans built a higher floor & used rooms as steam rooms. The Benedictines built the King's Bath above the sacred spring.
    Hot sunshine to end holiday. We visited Pouteney Bridge.

March 4th.
We've come to __Hamperley__, north of Ludlow. Simon got feasts for everyone out of the freezer when we arrived. Very hot sunny day.
    We've brough the old red push-truck for Charles to push – he's just got his first pair of size 3 shoes, – to try to help him stay upright.

Charles + John are thrilled to see the [cont.]
    Jini, Margaret + Helen arrived at 9.30. the next morning. They overshot the end of the road although Thomas waved frantically. Margaret + Helen have taught Charles to push the little red truck along, wearing his first pair of shoes.
    After coffee we set of up the Long Mynd. Many trees have blown over. We walked 5 miles, + up and down 700 ft. Simon carried Charles in the pack. The rest of us held John's hands, + sometimes gave him piggy packs. He managed very well.
    We had a picnic lunch watching the gliders being launched by a long [continues]

The valley walk down was very, very beautiful with running water all the way, sheep, some cows, & we paddled in the stream.

    There is an old, disused road which runs along one end of the campsite. It leads up to a tumble-down house with an inglenook & a pump in the garden.

    Simon cooked lamb chops on the barbque in the evening, after John & Charles had gone to bed. It was a very hot day.

    On the Sunday we walked along to road to St. Michael's church. It had a ~~patchwork~~ cross stitch sampler on the wall in memory of a parishoner. The rector was ill so there was no servic[e]

Thomas dashed back to the campsite to tell Grandma & Grandpa, they had gone to the farm, as Thomas discovered when he asked passerbys, "have you seen 2 people over 70?"

    We drove of to the Boiling Well along a precarious singe track road up the side of a hill "under 2 tons." Mist was sweeping across the moorland and it became very cold. So we moved on to lower ground for lunch. On the way [S]imon scraped the side of the van while passing a car.

    Margaret had made us a delicious lunch with salads & summer pudding.

John & Charles were too tired for another walk, so we rested in the van while the others walked around Robin Hood's Butts. It was warmer
	After tea we set off home, after the children had watched some model aeroplane flying.

May 30th 1990.  We have set off for __Dinas__ with Matt Portal, Claire 7 & Peter 4.  Jane is in Korea.
	On the way, Simon noticed the Welshpool steam railway. So at the next station, Eve & all the children went by train for 20 minutes to the terminus. Matt & Simon & Charles overtook them in the car & van. It was lovely & leafy & green after the hot van. We crossed a stream &

rode along by it. The train was smokey & smelly & had wooden seats.
	When we all met up we had a picnic lunch & drove on to Dinas.
	The next day we left on a tricky walk from the campsite – rocky – every footstep counted. Claire found it rather hard work. We had elevenses on the walk. Just when we had a clear view of the main road, we came to the top of a rise of rocks … and there was a deep, tree-filled gorge between us & the road. So we had to go back across a barbed-wire fence & find a way around it. Simon raced home with Charles on the pack to get lunch ready (leaving me to piggyback John) only to find the soup still frozen.

Catering for 10 per meal is quite a challenge, hampered by "Eve I don't like tomatoes/porkpies/chicken/dressing" etc. from Peter + Claire. However, they seem to be surviving on bread + cheese.
	On Friday we went to Harlech Castle. The rain bucketed down + we forgot to get out the gumboots. Feeling very cold we toured the Castle + Shops + descended yet again on the fish + chip shop "Eve, I like chips, but I don't like fish....."
	On Friday afternoon we gathered in "Matt's tent". Fortunately we can all just fit in the sitting-room part of it, around the grey table. Matt + Claire + Peter have the sleeping

pages.sketch_desc: Two hand-drawn sketches of tents, not a route map. Top sketch shows a dome tent labelled 'East Wind' with annotation 'Sleeps 2 boys, but could sleep 3.' Bottom sketch shows a larger frame/ridge tent labelled 'Cabanon' with annotations 'Sleeps 6 in back compartments.' and 'Room for grey table in sitting area'. The bottom sketch includes a small label that appears to read 'CABANON' on the tent bag/carry case at the base.
pages.text:
areas.
	So we now have 2 tents.

East Wind.

				Sleeps 2 boys,
				but could sleep 3.

[hand-drawn sketch of dome tent labelled "East Wind"]

Cabanon

				Sleeps 6 in
				back compartments.

				Room for grey
				table in
				sitting area

[hand-drawn sketch of frame tent labelled "Cabanon"]

Matt + the children are very good company - Matt smokes delicious-smelling cigars. There are several games of cricket with other children on the campsite too.
    On Saturday we go to the arts crafts village which has a very good acquarium, lobsters, octopi, trout, crabs. There is also a museum with sights + sounds of yesteryear - a blacksmiths forge, a carpenter /coffin-maker / stores. The RAF was stationed at Barmouth in WWII, so there's an aircraft museum too , + a children's playground.
    We returned to the van for lunch - the Portals perched on the back bumper with the doors hinged all the way back for the first time.

The Portals have left this afternoon, + Eve has been napping in the van with John + Charles while Simon takes Thomas, William + Peter on a very long walk - 2pm - 6 pm ? To be followed by a barbque?
    As well as the tent, we have added other supplies to the camping van this summer - a cutlery drainer, changing the double sleeping bag into 2 duvet-style quilts + fitted bottom sheet. Simon has enlarged the entrance to some of the cupboards + built a box to hold maps on his door. We now have 3 small grey bags for dirty clothes. Charles has a specially notified cardboard box to travel in so that he can't climb about.

June 3rd.

    We pack up at Dinas and set off for home.  We go north & have lunch at Celyn reservoir.
    On the way we pass some good views of Harlech Castle.  We also notice a good campsite on a promontary above Harlech - Merthyr Campsite.  A working farm with cows, __beautiful__ views across to Port Merion, very windy.  2½ mile walk down to the sea.  We may try it next year.
    In the afternoon we visit the highest waterfall in Wales, before reaching home.

June 23rd.

    Simon picked up Peter, Thomas & William from school & drove over to Long Buckby to pick up John, Charles & Eve from the station.  (We'd spent the afternoon visiting Rosemary Young.)
    After about 50 minutes more in the van we arrived at __Cherry Tree Farm__ **** campsite, Woodford Halse, which is within view of the Daventry post office tower.
    There is no pond here, superb views, a large field, no loos, hedges & trees, quite windy.  Peter lost his boomerang as soon as we arrived.  The farm house & walled

garden look Victorian. The garden is well-cared for, with lots of vegetables in rows. The chickens + sheep are very friendly. We stroked the sheep + one tried to chew my shorts. John was given bread by Simon to feed the chickens. He didn't realise that he should throw it. The chicken tried to jump up to his hand, which scared him.
    We walked about 4 miles in the morning to Woodford Halse to buy tea bags. The church dates from before 1201, + has a squint.
    On the way back it rained. We walked through fields of cows + down into a disused railway cutting with pigs + pig pens.

Cherry Tree Farm,
Woodford Halse.

pages.sketch_desc: Small hand-drawn triangle shape at bottom-left of the page, likely a simple sketch of a tent.
pages.text:
We started preparing lunch outside & had to eat it in the van. About 3 showers blew across, one of them very heavy, blowing rain horizontally & wetting the chairs under the table. We read books in the van & snoozed. After tea Simon went off for an hour's walk with Peter & Thomas
    We went to bed, Thomas & Peter in the tent. At 2.30 I over heard Thomas & Peter having an argument & finally the van door opened & Peter tottered in, soakily wet, sodden pyjamas & freezing cold. Thomas was wet too.
    Apparently the extra outer ground sheet had collected

the rainwater off the tent & the sleeping bags had soaked it up as it soaked in [illegible] through the groundsheet.
    The 4 of us tried to sleep shoulder to shoulder, but finally Simon got up & walked & read, leaving the 3 of us to sleep. We should have put T & P with their heads the other end.
    We went to church at Woodford Halse where they had 2 christenings. In the afternoon we saw the big House at Canons Ashby & joined the N.T.. Parts of the house & church feel very old, but [?18] modernisations. Beautiful tapestries & interesting rooms.

July 25 1990
Simon, Eve, Thomas, William, Peter
off to the Ardèche, France, & to
visit the Wegners in W. Germany.
	John & Charles looked after by
Nanny Helen, Jim, Margaret, Helen,
Rosemary, David & John Young
	We set off via Rugby, to take
phtos to Decorum. Lovely hot
sunny weather. We had lunch near
Rugby & Crick, on farmland. Simon
had been mending the hot tap &
forgotten to put the tap back on.
(We have now solved that by using
the on/off switch.) At 4.30. we
went through the Dartford Tunnel
with no delays. Half an hour later
we heard on the radio that there were
1½ mile queues in either direction
	Wednesday night we spent camped

on the road outside the Edwards'
house. Lee, Richard, Andrew 11,
Michael 9. It was [?Andrew's] Michael's last day
ever at primary school & he
went off to a party. Thomas,
William, Peter & Andrew played
football on the lawn & lego.
We had a delicious turkey supper
& tart. __Canterbury__, the day the new archbishop cho[se]
__Thursday__ We set off at 8.50.
& came across on the Pride of
Calais, big & clean. We had lunch
outside a farm barn near St. Omer,
then went on a motorway A26.
			Arras
				coffee &
				orangina in
				the square

We spent the night at __Gernicourt Campsite__ **** + called in to see the de [?Huellos] the next morning. Beatrice is getting married tomorrow. Madame is in bed with a bad back. When we arrived Charles-Henri was playing tennis with his cousin, Edouard. Charles-Henri suggested an exchange for our children. Alexi 12, Philippie 9, Herard 3 + Louis 3 months.
    We saw Reims Cathedral, arches, Chagall stained glass '74. William got a splinter.
    On to the south, past Clairvaux Abbey to a municipal campsite at __Arc en Barois__ ****. It's on an island, locked tennis court tennis wall, swings. We had a delicious meal at Hotel Place de Parc. William kept very quiet during his first course listening out for his lobster's cream [continues overleaf]

There was a thunderstorm before we ate + lots of rain, which cleared the air.
    __Saturday__
    The next morning Thomas set of for 5 croissants, refused to believe the woman when she presented them, + instead struggled back with 5 baguettes which lasted delicious. Along a lovely wooded road D6
    We were shopping for food + petrol on the outskirts of Dijon. We went into an apalling, huge supermarket, but got good food. The van was roasting + the fridge couldn't cope.
    Motoring south afterwards, through lyons + on to Tourion, it was 39°. We came to a campsite on __Doue River__, near __Colombiers te Vieux__ ****. We swam in the camp swimming pool.

42 __Sunday__
      We walked to a village for church, but arrived too late. Père Vigne had started a convent there. Lots of Stations of the cross by a modern sculptor Bongati? Simon ran home because it started raining. We had very heavy rain during lunch. In the afternoon we walked back to the river & the boys & Simon swam, & so did Eve after washing all the clothes in the river. A glorious afternoon. In the evening Simon cooked spaghetti & it rained again. There are beautiful brown & white rabbits in the campsite, under poplar trees. The tree leaves have stained the tent because of all the rain.
__Monday__
      We motored across the north of the Ardèche, looking at old volcanoes. The mountainous

countryside is very pretty, but slow motoring. We have bought lavender honey at __Gerbier de Jonc__.
      We are camping by a volcanic lake at __Lac de Issarlès__. Pine trees, a beautiful view & swimming in the lake.
__Tuesday__ We stopped at __Aubenas__ to see the château. It was lived in continuously by the same family (with 2 or 3 female inheritors) from about 1200 to 1792. The boys were very patient, considering they couldn't understand a word of the French guide. A lovely portrait of a woman in the gallery at the top.
      We came on to __Les Blaches__ Casteljou. It would be even more beautiful if it weren't so crowded.

There are lots of white outcrops of rock, we're on a sort of island, the boys keep finding new paths between the loos & the van. Old limestone. People probably lived in these rocks once. Amazing gorge, with striated rocky cliffs on either side.
__Wednesday__ The boys swom in the morning - marvellous rocks to jump off & pebbly beach. In the afternoon we hired 2 canoes & took a picnic lunch. The van drove us up river & we paddled our canoes 12 kilometres back to the camp. Fortunately the canoe-launchers also gave us a watertight plastic drum, because Simon & Thomas capsized at the first rapids. It was great fun going downstream.

In the end it became easier to steer through the rapids, when we knew what to anticipate. One campsite had built a dam to provide a swimming pool for the campers, after that the water in the river was rather low & we had to push the canoes over the last 3 rapids. Thomas & William became very proficient in their canoe together. The French children enjoyed "bombing" us as we passed near their rocks.
__Thursday__
    We went swimming & then Peter felt unwell, & then Eve had a tummy bug & felt sick all day. Simon & Thomas went out for a

2 hour walk in the evening.
Friday we packed up & left. & went to see Madame de Nayelle's mother's château, which she sold about 8 years ago - __Chambonais__. A fairy tale castle, with shining turrets & a beautiful garden 'les terraces' with water in a moat at one end of the garden. It is now a hotel & the gardens are overgrown. The village is one of the loveliest we've seen, very old & unspoilt. An old lady (Madam [?Lagrage]) who knows the Nayelle's well, was sweeping around the church & sold us peaches, strawberries, apple juice. The church is old, with head gargoyles, & one of a person's bum on the loo.
   Thomas bought a boat at __Joyeuse__ & we started driving down the River Ardèche (which joins the Rhone) to camp

pages.sketch_desc: A pen sketch occupying most of the page. The label 'Aigueze' appears at the top-left. The upper portion shows a detailed drawing of a village skyline: a church with a tall spire on the left, rows of buildings/houses, and what appears to be castle ruins or fortified walls on the right. Below the village, there is a sketch of steep cliffs or rocky gorge walls. At the bottom, the scene shows a river with small figures of people in boats/canoes on the water, with rocks visible in the river.
pages.text: Aigueze

Beautiful view across the river Ardèche to Aiguèze with crenellated walls on towers + church. 3 church bells dong the hours. Huge cliffs + the river bllow! The boys played on Thomas' boat in the river. Peter jumped of very high rocks + then Thomas did too, with Jamie + Sophie Walker. Their father is a P.E. instructor at Telford. Peter hurt his toe. Mr. Walker took 2 days in May to do the rapids on the Ardèche with his college, but we set out with the boys to do it in one.
	We camped at Révire ***** + went to visit caves on __Saturday__ at St. Marcel Grottos. Very well lit + blissfully cool.
	When we returned the canoe hire

shop at our campsite let TWP borrow kayaks + practise paddling.
	Then we set off for supper at Aiguèze. This involved swimming — or wading — across the river, while Thomas rowed all our clothes + bags (safely!) across in his boat. We looked around the lovely town, but only for a short time, + then had supper. With the help of William's new torch + a beautiful full moon we made it safely home.

__Sunday__ A fantastic day. We set off at 9, had the canoes in the water at Chames by 11 + finally got home at 8.30, just before sun set, thank goodness. Some of the rapids were pretty difficult, + the capsizing account is Simon 3 Thomas 2 William 2, Peter 2, Eve 1. When Simon capsized he would drag the boat — and very willing crew — back

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn map of the Ardèche river route. At the top, a volcanic lake labelled 'Le lac d'Issarlès' with a sketch of a mountain peak labelled 'Gerbier de Jonc'. To the upper right, 'Col de Vieux [?Dombier]' with notes about washing clothes in river and a swim pool at campsite. A sinuous river line labelled 'R. Ardèche' runs from Aubenas (top, with a dot) southward. Joyeuse is marked to the left mid-river. Chassezac joins from the left near Chaubon(as), annotated '5 hour canoes'. Lower right shows Chames with '4 hour canoes', then caves, and at the bottom Gr St. Martial and St. Martial d'Ardèche. Arrow pointing north at top. Campsite/château sketch near Chaubon(as).

Thunder + rain)

(¼ size Wales)

[?Dombier]

(very hot, desert-like.)

up the rapids to try again. Eve was exceedingly proud of her non-capsizing record almost to the end of the trip, when she + Peter tipped over + were rescued by a naked man from the Naturist beaches. Peter was very brave, quite fearless about the next rapids. Eve lost a shoe, but found someone else's straight away.
   By 4 o'clock Simon found that he had misread the map + we were only ½ way back to base, not ¾, as he had thought. Fortunately we (Simon) had brought far more food + drink than we needed. Thomas was very tired. William got blisters. At times there was a bad head wind.
   When we got triumphantly home we went out for steak + chips; it was a children's restaurant, so they played games + we had icecreams while Simon went home with[...]

53 __Monday__ Thomas + William had a swim before breakfast while Simon + Peter bought bread. We left early + drove over 400 miles north to near Germany, beyond Metz.
      We looked at __Thionville__ campsite which was so overcrowded that tents were pegged inches apart. As we had been on the road for 11 hours we decided to just have supper + hope for the best. Eve asked a woman walking her dog where we should eat + she suggested "Vinci". It was superb. We then set off into the night to a wood Simon had seen on the map 5 miles n.e.. Simon immediately found an isolated field which made an idyllic overnight stop ****.
__Tuesday__ [strikethrough: See] We got ready to leave in [?to] 2 hours + stopped in [?Trion]

54 After cappucini in the square, we looked at the Cathedral + joined in some ecumenical prayers. The arches in the side aisles are glorious [strikethrough: A+A] pink + grey stone — same as the colours in the van interior. The marble + stone carving around the [strikethrough: &] altar + lectern is nice too.
      Thomas + William + Peter are most impressed by the automation in the motor-way stop loos — you just touch a pad for flushing. It's certainly a change from the French holes-in-the-floor.
      On to the Wagners. for 2 nights, at __Bad Honnef__
      Simon + Klaus + Anne went out to supper (Eve again ill.)

In the morning we walked into Bad Honnef + the boys spent their pocket money. Annie took us up the cog railway in the afternoon to the of the "[?7n] Hills", the Dragonsfeld. A million people a year visit it. Lovely views of the Rhine up to Bonn, + beyond to Cologne on a clear day. We walked down.

__Thursday__
    We walked along the Rhein, crossed [to an] island + took 2 ferries with Patrick. André was at school + [F]ather stayed at home to wait with Annie. [I]'ve bought some Birkenstock sandals at the factory on the way back to the Wegners' house, while the boys played in a huge tub of balls.
    After lunch we set off for Cala[is]
